text.body_text:
that exploited rhythm, meter, and music, achieved the desired psychological response in the listener. Listeners could memorize with greater ease what was sung than what was said. Plato attacked this method because it discouraged disputation and argument. It was in his opinion the chief obstacle to abstract, speculative reasoning—he called it "a poison, and an enemy of the people."

"Blind," all-hearing Homer inherited this metaphorical mode of speech, a speech which, like a prism, refracts much meaning to a single point.

"Precision" is sacrificed for a greater degree of suggestion. Myth is the mode of simultaneous awareness of a complex group of causes and effects.

Electric circuitry'confers a mythic dimension on our ordinary individual and group actions. Our technology forces us to live mythically, but we continue to think fragmentarily, and on single, separate planes.

Myth means putting on the audience, putting on one's environment. The Beatles do this. They are a group of people who suddenly were able to put on their audience and the English language with musical effects—putting on a whole vesture, a whole time, a Zeit.

Young people are looking for a formula for putting on the universe—participation mystique. They do not look for detached patterns—for ways of relating themselves to the world, a la nineteenth century.

A noted publisher in Chicago reports there is a simple technique for acquiring a powerful memory which can pay you real dividends in both business and social advancement and works like magic to give you added poise, necessary self-confidence and greater popularity.

According to this publisher, many people do not realize how much they could influence others simply by remembering accurately everything they see, hear, or read. Whether in business, at social functions or even in casual conversations with new acquaintances, there are ways in which you can dominate each situation by your ability to remember.

To acquaint the readers of this paper with the easy-to-follow rules for developing skill in remembering anything you choose to remember, the publishers have printed full details of their self-training method in a new book, "Adventures in Memory," which will be; mailed free to anyone who requests it. No obligation. Send your name, address and zip code to: Memory Studies, 835 Diversey Parkway, Dept. 8183, Chicago, Ill. 60614. A postcard will do.

design.layout_description: A stark, two-column layout defined by the gutter. The left page contains a single, justified column of McLuhan's prose. The right page, under the heading 'EDUCATION,' features a large, italic headline followed by a ragged-right column of text formatted like a mail-order advertisement. The two pages are compositionally separate but thematically juxtaposed.

typography.body_font_style: Serif font, standard book size. The left page text is justified, while the right page text is ragged-right, mimicking advertisement copy.
typography.display_font_style: Right page features a large, elegant, italicized serif headline ('Develop A Powerful Memory?'). Above it is a smaller, all-caps, sans-serif section title ('EDUCATION').

color_and_tone.description: Standard black text on a white page, creating maximum clarity and contrast.

design.left_right_relationship: Ironic juxtaposition. The left page presents McLuhan's complex, contemporary argument about mythic, holistic thinking in the electric age. The right page presents a found artifact of print culture: a simplistic, mechanistic advertisement for rote memorization, framed as an obsolete approach to 'education.'

rhetoric.argument: The new mode of 'education' required by the electric age is a 'participation mystique' involving holistic, mythic awareness, which is fundamentally opposed to the old, print-based model of education that emphasized fragmented knowledge and rote memorization. The advertisement on the right is presented as a relic of this obsolete, linear way of thinking.
rhetoric.strategy_description: The entire argument is constructed through the ironic juxtaposition of two texts. McLuhan's theoretical prose on the left, discussing myth and The Beatles, is placed against a found mail-order advertisement for a memory course. The reader is forced to compare the two modes of thought—holistic participation versus linear memorization—and conclude that the latter is comically outdated.
rhetoric.reader_experience: The reader experiences an intellectual 'aha' moment, recognizing the ironic contrast between the two pages. The advertisement is reframed as a historical artifact, prompting a feeling of superiority or knowingness about the shift in consciousness that McLuhan is describing.

themes.mapping_rationale: The spread's contrast between holistic, mythic participation (left) and fragmented, linear memorization (right) directly parallels contemporary debates in education. The 'powerful memory' ad represents an outdated model of knowledge acquisition, much like how rote learning is challenged by the availability of search engines and AI. The core tension is between internalizing information versus navigating a total information environment, a key issue in 'attention_and_cognition'.

progression.relationship_to_previous: Continues the exploration of how electric media reshape sensory and cognitive patterns, following spreads that discuss the shift from visual to acoustic space.
progression.relationship_to_next: This critique of old educational models sets the stage for further examination of specific institutions and social structures being transformed by new media, such as the family and the job.

_array_response.notes: The advertisement on the right is a found object, a piece of ephemera from print culture used to make a rhetorical point. Its specificity (Chicago address, book title 'Adventures in Memory') grounds the abstract argument on the left in a tangible, almost mundane reality.
